# COURSE: Tư tưởng Hồ Chí Minh (BAA00003)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: BAA00003
- **English Name**: Ho Chi Minh’s Ideology
- **Vietnamese Name**: Tư tưởng Hồ Chí Minh
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Ngô Tuấn Phương
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 8
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 25 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 0 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 57.5 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Introduction to the Subject
- Chapter 1: Concepts, Subjects, Research Methods, and the Importance of Studying Ho Chi Minh's Thought
- Chapter 2: Foundations, Formation Process, and Development of Ho Chi Minh's Thought
- Chapter 3: Ho Chi Minh's Thought on National Independence Associated with Socialism
- Chapter 4: Ho Chi Minh's Thought on the Communist Party of Vietnam and the State of the People, By the People, and For the People
- Chapter 5: Ho Chi Minh's Thought on National Unity and International Solidarity
- Chapter 6: Ho Chi Minh's Thought on Culture, Ethics, and Humanity
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:


## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Present the concept of Ho Chi Minh's thought and the basic ideas, arguments, and viewpoints that emerged during the process of forming and developing Ho Chi Minh's Thought. Learners can analyze and explain the fundamental content, thoughts, and viewpoints of Ho Chi Minh's Thought. Apply the values of Ho Chi Minh's Thought in their lives. Develop and enhance the skills of analysis, synthesis, and systematization of the knowledge that learners have acquired
- G2.Apply the values of Ho Chi Minh's Thought in their lives. Develop and enhance the skills of analysis, synthesis, and systematization of the knowledge that learners have acquired
- G3.Develop and enhance learners' skills in commenting, evaluating, and comparing the content they have studied for practical application.
• Content:
- Introduction to the Subject
- Chapter 1: Concepts, Subjects, Research Methods, and the Importance of Studying Ho Chi Minh's Thought
- Chapter 2: Foundations, Formation Process, and Development of Ho Chi Minh's Thought
- Chapter 3: Ho Chi Minh's Thought on National Independence Associated with Socialism
- Chapter 4: Ho Chi Minh's Thought on the Communist Party of Vietnam and the State of the People, By the People, and For the People
- Chapter 5: Ho Chi Minh's Thought on National Unity and International Solidarity
- Chapter 6: Ho Chi Minh's Thought on Culture, Ethics, and Humanity
• Examination forms: Open Test, Final examination, Group Presentation
• Study and examination requirements:
- All students are responsible for reading and following strictly the regulations and policies of the school and university.
- Students who are absent for more than 3 theory sessions are not allowed to take the exams.
- For any kind of cheating and plagiarism, students will be graded 0 for the course. The incident is then submitted to the school and university for further review.
- Students are encouraged to form study groups to discuss on the topics. However, individual work must be done and submitted on your own.
- Regular class attendance is strongly advised and is necessary for students to fully grasp many of the course concepts.
- Please be on time to class.
- If you miss a class session, it will be your responsibility to find out the materials that were covered.
- Students in attendance are expected to be active participants in the course. The participation includes contributing to class discussions, providing insight into the class discussion topics, raising questions, and relating class material to personal experiences and other course topics.


## COURSE TOPICS & CONTENT
- Moodle and e-mail will be used to communicate with students and disseminate materials and assignments throughout the course. So, students should check Moodle and their email at least once per day.
- When sending e-mail to the instructor, please begin the Subject of the message with the following format: [Course ID]. For example, "[CSC10001] Ask for additional materials".

## POLICIES & REQUIREMENTS
- All students are responsible for reading and following strictly the regulations and policies of the school and university.
- Students who are absent for more than 3 theory sessions are not allowed to take the exams.
- For any kind of cheating and plagiarism, students will be graded 0 for the course. The incident is then submitted to the school and university for further review.
- Students are encouraged to form study groups to discuss on the topics. However, individual work must be done and submitted on your own.
- Regular class attendance is strongly advised and is necessary for students to fully grasp many of the course concepts.
- Please be on time to class.
- If you miss a class session, it will be your responsibility to find out the materials that were covered.
- Students in attendance are expected to be active participants in the course. The participation includes contributing to class discussions, providing insight into the class discussion topics, raising questions, and relating class material to personal experiences and other course topics.


## READING MATERIALS
- [1]Tư tưởng Hồ Chí Minh,Bộ Giáo dục và Đào tạo,2019,Nxb. Chính trị quốc gia, Hà Nội.
Hà Nội.

# COURSE: Pháp luật đại cương (BAA00004)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: BAA00004
- **English Name**: Introduction to Law
- **Vietnamese Name**: Pháp luật đại cương
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Hoàng Thanh Tú
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 2
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 0 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 86.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Chapter 1: General Introduction to the State and Law
- Lesson 1. The Origin, Nature, Characteristics, and Functions of the State.
- Lesson 2. The Origin, Nature, Characteristics, and Role of Law
- Chapter2: The State Apparatus.
- Lesson 3. Type of State, Form of State.
- Lesson 4. The State Apparatus of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam.
- Chapter 3: Law – A Tool for Regulating Legal Relations.
- Lesson 5. Legal Norms, Legal Relations, and the System of Legal Normative Documents in Vietnam.
- Lesson 6. Implementation of the Law, Violation of the Law, and Legal Awareness
- Chapter 4: Branches of Law in the Legal System of Vietnam
- Lesson 7: Constitutional Law
- Lesson 8: Civil Law
- Lesson 9: Civil Procedure Law
- Lesson 10: Criminal Law
- Lesson 11: Civil Procedure Law
- Chapter 5: General issues of anti-corruption
- Lesson 12. Concept and classification of corrupt behaviors
- Lesson 13. Dealing with corrupt behaviors, responsibilities of individuals, agencies, and organizations in anti-corruption efforts
- Review
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Pháp luật đại cương,Tập thể tác giả Trường Đại học Luật TP.HCM,2019,

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Present the basic system of knowledge about the state and law; the main content of some major branches of law.
- G2.Apply the basic knowledge of the state and law in cognitive activities and practical activities
• Content:
- Chapter 1: General Introduction to the State and Law
- Lesson 1. The Origin, Nature, Characteristics, and Functions of the State.
- Lesson 2. The Origin, Nature, Characteristics, and Role of Law
- Chapter2: The State Apparatus.
- Lesson 3. Type of State, Form of State.
- Lesson 4. The State Apparatus of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam.
- Chapter 3: Law – A Tool for Regulating Legal Relations.
- Lesson 5. Legal Norms, Legal Relations, and the System of Legal Normative Documents in Vietnam.
- Lesson 6. Implementation of the Law, Violation of the Law, and Legal Awareness
- Chapter 4: Branches of Law in the Legal System of Vietnam
- Lesson 7: Constitutional Law
- Lesson 8: Civil Law
- Lesson 9: Civil Procedure Law
- Lesson 10: Criminal Law
- Lesson 11: Civil Procedure Law
- Chapter 5: General issues of anti-corruption
- Lesson 12. Concept and classification of corrupt behaviors
- Lesson 13. Dealing with corrupt behaviors, responsibilities of individuals, agencies, and organizations in anti-corruption efforts
- Review
• Examination forms: Close Test, Final examination, Homework, In-class Activities, Quiz, quick test, Mid-term examination
• Study and examination requirements:
- All students are responsible for reading and following strictly the regulations and policies of the school and university.

# COURSE: Kinh tế đại cương (BAA00005)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: BAA00005
- **English Name**: Introduction to Economics
- **Vietnamese Name**: Kinh tế đại cương
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Dr. Nguyễn Thị Đông
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 4
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 25 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 0 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 57.5 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Chapter 1: Introduction (2 periods)
- Chapter 2: Supply, Demand, and Market Prices (8 periods)
- Supply and Demand
- Market Equilibrium
- Government Intervention Failures
- Chapter 3: Consumer Behavior (6 periods)
- Indifference Curves and Consumer Utility
- Budget Constraints
- Consumer Choice
- Chapter 4: Theory of Production Firms (6 periods)
- Production and Production Costs
- Perfectly Competitive Markets
- Monopoly Markets
- Chapter 5: Measuring National Output, Aggregate Supply, and Aggregate Demand (8 periods)
- Methods for Calculating National Output Indicators
- Price Issues in Calculating National Output Indicators
- Aggregate Supply, Aggregate Demand, and Equilibrium Output
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Nguyên lý kinh tế học [Principles of economics] (2nd edn). NewYork: Worth Publisher.,Mankiw, N.G,2003,Bản dịch của Khoa Kinh Tế Học, Trường Đại Học Kinh Tế Quốc Dân. NXB Thống Kê.
- [2]Kinh tế học vi mô,Giáo trình dùng trong các trường đại học, cao đẳng khối Kinh tế.,2003,NXB Giáo Dục
- [3]Kinh tế học vĩ mô,Giáo trình dùng trong các trường đại học, cao đẳng khối Kinh tế.,2002,NXB Giáo Dục

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Apply economic concepts, principles, and tools to analyze economic issues.
- G2.Understand the nature of various issues and phenomena discussed in the media, both micro and macro, such as national accounting indicators like GDP, GNP, and certain economic indexes.
- G3.Analyze price fluctuations in both domestic and international markets. The course will also foster independent thinking through individual assignments and in-class discussions.
- G4.Apply economic knowledge to analyze, synthesize, and evaluate how government economic policies might affect consumers and markets.
• Content:
- Chapter 1: Introduction (2 periods)
- Chapter 2: Supply, Demand, and Market Prices (8 periods)
- Supply and Demand
- Market Equilibrium
- Government Intervention Failures
- Chapter 3: Consumer Behavior (6 periods)
- Indifference Curves and Consumer Utility
- Budget Constraints
- Consumer Choice
- Chapter 4: Theory of Production Firms (6 periods)
- Production and Production Costs
- Perfectly Competitive Markets
- Monopoly Markets
- Chapter 5: Measuring National Output, Aggregate Supply, and Aggregate Demand (8 periods)
- Methods for Calculating National Output Indicators
- Price Issues in Calculating National Output Indicators
- Aggregate Supply, Aggregate Demand, and Equilibrium Output
• Examination forms: Close Test, Final examination, Homework, In-class Activities, Multiple Choice, Excercise, Mid-term examination
• Study and examination requirements:
- All students are responsible for reading and following strictly the regulations and policies of the school and university.
- Students who are absent for more than 3 theory sessions are not allowed to take the exams.
- For any kind of cheating and plagiarism, students will be graded 0 for the course. The incident is then submitted to the school and university for further review.
- Students are encouraged to form study groups to discuss on the topics. However, individual work must be done and submitted on your own.
- Regular class attendance is strongly advised and is necessary for students to fully grasp many of the course concepts.
- Please be on time to class.
- If you miss a class session, it will be your responsibility to find out the materials that were covered.
- Students in attendance are expected to be active participants in the course. The participation includes contributing to class discussions, providing insight into the class discussion topics, raising questions, and relating class material to personal experiences and other course topics.

# COURSE: Tâm lý đại cương (BAA00006)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: BAA00006
- **English Name**: Introduction to Psychology
- **Vietnamese Name**: Tâm lý đại cương
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Kim Thị Dung
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 4
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 25 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 0 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 57.5 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Issue 1: General Overview of Psychology
- Issue 2: Subject, Tasks, and Research Methods
- Issue 3: Natural and Social Foundations of the Formation of Psychology and Consciousness
- Issue 4: Sensory Cognitive Processes
- Issue 5: Rational Cognitive Processes
- Issue 6: Memory and Attention
- Issue 7: Emotions and Feelings
- Issue 8: Willpower
- Issue 9: Personality
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Đề cương bài giảng môn Tâm lý học đại cương,Phạm Văn Dinh, Lê Văn Bích,,Đại học Luật, Tp.HCM.
- [2]Giáo trình tâm lý học đại cương,Đặng Thanh Nga (Chủ biên),2006,NXB Công an nhân dân, Hà Nội
- [3]Giáo trình tâm lý học đại cương,Nguyễn Quang Uẩn (Chủ biên),2005,NXB Đại học sư phạm, Hà Nội

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G3.Understand the role and functions of psychological phenomena.
- G1.Understand the nature, mechanisms of emergence, and formation of human psychological phenomena."
- G2.Identify and explain the characteristic features, specific manifestations, and the relationships between basic psychological phenomena of human beings."
• Content:
- Issue 1: General Overview of Psychology
- Issue 2: Subject, Tasks, and Research Methods
- Issue 3: Natural and Social Foundations of the Formation of Psychology and Consciousness
- Issue 4: Sensory Cognitive Processes
- Issue 5: Rational Cognitive Processes
- Issue 6: Memory and Attention
- Issue 7: Emotions and Feelings
- Issue 8: Willpower
- Issue 9: Personality

# COURSE: Phương pháp luận sáng tạo (BAA00007)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: BAA00007
- **English Name**: Creativity Methodologies
- **Vietnamese Name**: Phương pháp luận sáng tạo
- **Responsible Person**: for the module:
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 4
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 25 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 0 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 57.5 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Chapter 1: Introduction (2 periods)
- Some basic concepts.
- Subjects, objectives, benefits, and significance of the course on creative methodology..
- The science of creativity and creative methodology: a brief history from Heuristics to Creatology.
- Chapter 2: Natural Methods for Problem Solving and Decision Making – Overview of Approaches to Building Creative Methodology – TRIZ (2 periods).
- Trial and error method.
- Advantages and disadvantages of the trial and error method.
- Classification of levels of creativity and levels of difficulty of problems.
- Overview of approaches in the field of creative methodology.
- Theory of solving inventive problems (TRIZ): basic ideas and sources of knowledge.
- Block diagram of the theory of solving inventive problems (TRIZ).
- Chapter 3: Some scientific and technical knowledge as the foundation of the subject (12 periods)
- From needs to actions and vice versa.
- Model of transforming information into knowledge in the process of problem-solving and decision-making.
- Psychological factors and processes in creative thinking.
- Psychological inertia.
- Materialist dialectics and dialectical thinking.
- Types of contradictions in problem-solving and decision-making.
- Systems and systems thinking.
- System inertia.
- Regarding the two concepts: invention and innovation. The role and benefits of patent information in building creative methodology.
- Chapter 4: Basic creative tricks (principles) (12 periods)
- Things to note about basic creative tricks (principles).
- Text presenting the basic creative principles.
- Program for discovering tricks and reconstructing the thinking process to achieve the intended objects.
- Program for streamlining the problem-solving and decision-making process.
- Table of principles used to solve technical contradictions.
- System of model transformations used to solve physical contradictions.
- From principles to methods.
- Chapter 5: Methods for enhancing creative thinking (6 periods)
- Method of Focal Objects.
- Morphological Analysis method.
- Check-listing Method.
- Brainstorming method.
- Synectics method (using analogies).
- Chapter 6: Laws of system development (10 periods)
- Laws of system development.
- The lifecycle of a system.
- Diagram of the potential developments of a system.
- Creative principles and methods: specific representations of the laws of system development.
- Chapter 7: Summary of the introductory program on creative methodology (1 period)
- Reasons for economic success in the 19th and 20th centuries.
- Reasons for economic success in the 21st century.
- Challenges.
- Consequences of the challenges.
- Requirements and actions needed to cope with the challenges.
- New skills for the future.

- G1.Provide a system of ways to consider phenomena.
- G2.Enhance observation skills and creative curiosity.
- G3.Analyze and logically explain existing creative solutions.
- G4.Increase sensitivity in acquiring and evaluating the value of information.
- G5.Recognize the unified similarities among systems that seem very different.
- G6.Overcome psychological inertia.
- G7.Help identify available reserves in the system, especially free reserves that can be utilized.
- G8.Propose and select appropriate approaches to solve problems.
- G9.Resolve conflicts present in the problem.
- G10.Generate ideas for improving the given system.
- G11.Forecast the developmental trends of the given system in the future.
- G12.Assist in identifying, formulating, and selecting the problems that need to be solved.
- G13.Practice developing creative imagination.
- G14.Improve oneself, establish a systematic, creative work style and mindset.
- G15.Contribute to building systematic and dialectical thinking.

- G5.Practice basic writing skills: Students will learn how to write short paragraphs, emails, or simple essays on familiar topics.
- G6.Boost confidence in using English: Foster confidence when using English, helping students feel more comfortable expressing themselves in the language.

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Enhance communication skills in English: Develop advanced listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills. Students will improve their ability to communicate in academic and professional situations.
- G2.Improve academic writing skills: Focus on writing essays, reports, or other academic texts clearly and with proper structure. Students will learn to argue, analyze, and present their viewpoints logically.
- G3.Develop presentation skills: Train students to present ideas and give public presentations in English confidently and professionally.
- G4.Expand vocabulary and grammar knowledge: Improve specialized, technical, and academic vocabulary while reinforcing grammar knowledge for accurate application in real-life communication situations.
- G5.Enhance listening comprehension and reflexes: Develop the ability to understand complex lectures, conversations, and listening materials. Students will become familiar with various English accents and learn to respond quickly.
- G6.Prepare for international English exams: In some programs, English 4 may aim to help students prepare for English proficiency exams such as TOEFL, IELTS, or similar tests, which require a higher level of English proficiency.
- G7.Develop teamwork skills: Students may participate in group projects, discussions, and collaboration with classmates to complete academic tasks, thereby improving their teamwork skills in English.

# COURSE: Triết học Mác - Lênin (BAA00101)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: BAA00101
- **English Name**: Marxist-Leninist Philosophy
- **Vietnamese Name**: Triết học Mác - Lênin
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Ngô Tuấn Phương
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 4
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 0 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 86.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Introduction to the Subject
- Chapter 1: Philosophy and the Role of Philosophy in Social Life
- Chapter 2: Dialectical Materialism
- Chapter 3: Historical Materialism
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Giáo trình Triết học Mác – Lênin,Bộ Giáo dục và Đào tạo,2019,Nxb. Chính trị quốc gia, Hà Nội.
- [2]Bộ Giáo dục và Đào tạo (2012), Giáo trình Những Nguyên lý cơ bản của chủ nghĩa Mác – Lênin, Nxb. Chính trị quốc gia, Hà Nội.,,,
- [3]Giáo trình Triết học Mác-Lênin,Hội đồng Trung ương,2008,Nxb. Chính trị quốc gia, Hà Nội

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Understand the basic knowledge of philosophy in general and the conditions for the emergence of Marxist-Leninist philosophy. At the same time, assist students in analyzing and evaluating the essence of the revolution in philosophy carried out by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, as well as the stages of the formation and development of Marxist-Leninist philosophy. Additionally, emphasize the role of Marxist philosophy in social life and in the modern era.
- G2.Understand the dialectical materialist view of matter, the forms and modes of existence of matter, the origin and nature of consciousness, and the dialectical relationship between matter and consciousness. Assist students in understanding the fundamental contents of dialectical materialism, as well as its methodological significance in cognition and practice. Help students grasp the basic knowledge of the theory of cognition in dialectical materialism and its methodological significance."
- G3.Understand the knowledge of the socio-economic formation theory and apply its methodological significance to the practical context of Vietnam. Assist students in understanding the basic viewpoints of Marxist-Leninist philosophy regarding classes, the state, social revolution, nations, and the relationships among classes, nations, and humanity, as well as the methodological significance in comprehending the fundamental issues of the Vietnamese revolution. Help students grasp the fundamental viewpoints of Marxist-Leninist philosophy regarding human beings, social consciousness, and its application to the Vietnamese revolution."
- G8.Analyze the scientific and revolutionary foundations of dialectical materialism; and struggle against the erroneous views of idealism and metaphysical materialism.
- G9.Adopt a Marxist stance, reinforce their belief in the scientific and revolutionary nature of historical materialism, trust in the Party's policies and viewpoints, and struggle against hostile and erroneous opinions to defend the ideological foundation of the Party.
• Content:
- Introduction to the Subject
- Chapter 1: Philosophy and the Role of Philosophy in Social Life
- Chapter 2: Dialectical Materialism
- Chapter 3: Historical Materialism

# COURSE: Kinh tế chính trị Mác - Lênin (BAA00102)

## GENERAL INFORMATION

- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 5

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Introduction to the Subject
- Chapter 1: Subject, Research Methods, and Functions of Marxist-Leninist Political Economy
- Chapter 2: Commodities, Markets, and the Roles of Market Participants
- Chapter 3: Surplus Value in a Market Economy
- Chapter 4: Competition and Monopoly in a Market Economy
- Chapter 5: Socialist-Oriented Market Economy and Economic Interest Relations in Vietnam
- Chapter 6: Industrialization, Modernization, and Vietnam's Integration into the Global Economy
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G2.Summarize the system of viewpoints and economic laws. Evaluate and recognize the nature of economic interests in the market economy.

- G3.Address practical issues that arise. Distinguish right from wrong and engage in critical thinking. Choose practical activities based on the methodology of Marxism-Leninism.

# COURSE: Chủ nghĩa xã hội khoa học (BAA00103)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: BAA00103
- **English Name**: Scientific Socialism
- **Vietnamese Name**: Chủ nghĩa xã hội khoa học
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Dr. Nguyễn Đình Quốc Cường
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 6
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 25 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 0 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 57.5 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Chapter 1: Introduction to Scientific Socialism
- Chapter 2: The Historical Mission of the Working Class
- Chapter 3: Socialism and the Transitional Period to Socialism
- Chapter 4: Socialist Democracy and the Socialist State
- Chapter 5: Social Structure - Classes and Class Alliances, Stratification During the Transitional Period to Socialism
- Chapter 6: Ethnic and Religious Issues During the Transitional Period to Socialism
- Chapter 7: Family Issues During the Transitional Period to Socialism
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Giáo trình Chủ nghĩa xã hội khoa học,Bộ Giáo dục và Đào tạo,2019,Nxb. Chính trị quốc gia, Hà Nội.
- [2]Giáo trình Những Nguyên lý cơ bản của chủ nghĩa Mác – Lênin,Bộ Giáo dục và Đào tạo,2012,Nxb. Chính trị quốc gia, Hà Nội.

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Understand and systematically analyze the basic contents of the scientific socialism course.
- G3.Understand the scientific grounds for combating distorted perceptions and the sabotage by hostile forces.
- G4.Apply the knowledge of scientific socialism to evaluate and solve political and social issues arising in practice.
- G5.Apply the methodological principles derived from the theoretical content of dialectical materialism to cognition and practice
- G2.Analyze and evaluate the inevitable emergence of socialism, as well as the tasks and solutions that need to be implemented in the process of building socialism in general and in Vietnam in particular.
- G5.Apply the scientific viewpoints of Marxism-Leninism to identify the schemes of peaceful evolution by reactionary and hostile forces.
- G6.Assess the scientific and revolutionary nature of Marxism-Leninism in general and scientific socialism in particular."
- G7.Analyze the goals, ideals of socialism, and the path to socialism.
- G8.Analyze and evaluate the success of the renewal process initiated and led by the Communist Party of Vietnam.

- Chapter 2: The Historical Mission of the Working Class

# COURSE: Lịch sử Đảng Cộng sản Việt Nam (BAA00104)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: BAA00104
- **English Name**: History of Vietnamese Communist Party
- **Vietnamese Name**: Lịch sử Đảng Cộng sản Việt Nam
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Ngô Tuấn Phương
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 7
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 25 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 0 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 57.5 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Chapter 1: The Birth of the Communist Party of Vietnam and Its Leadership in the Struggle for Power (1930-1945)
- Chapter 2: The Party Leads Two Resistance Wars, Achieving National Liberation and Unification of the Country (1945-1975)
- Chapter 3: The Party Leads the Entire Nation in the Transition to Socialism and the Implementation of the Renovation Process (1975-2018)
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Chương trình môn học Lịch sử Đảng Cộng sản Việt Nam,Bộ Giáo dục và Đào tạo,2019,
- [2]Giáo trình quốc gia các môn khoa học Mác – Lênin, Tư tưởng Hồ Chí Minh (2018), Giáo trình Lịch sử Đảng Cộng sản Việt Nam (tái bản có sửa chữa, bổ sung),Hội đồng Trung ương,2018,Nxb. Chính trị quốc gia, Hà Nội.

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G6.Apply the methodological principles derived from the theoretical content of historical materialism to analyze the understanding and creative application of the Communist Party of Vietnam in the practical context of the Vietnamese revolution.
- G1.Summarize the inevitable process of the formation of the Communist Party of Vietnam and the Party's first political platform.
- G2.Describe the Party's leadership process through different periods: from 1930 to 1945; from 1945 to 1975; and from 1975 to the present.
- G3.Generalize and interpret the development of the Party's policies and leadership during the period when the whole country transitioned to socialism and implemented the renovation process from after national reunification in 1975 to the present.
- G4.Identify the Party's thinking on industrialization to demonstrate issues or events related to the industrialization of the country.
- G5.Identify the Party's thinking on the market economy to demonstrate issues or events related to the country's economy.
- G6.Identify the Party's thinking on culture and society to demonstrate issues or events related to the country.
- G7.Identify the Party's thinking on foreign affairs to demonstrate issues or events related to the country's foreign relations.
- G8.Share a proper understanding of the Party's leadership in the Vietnamese revolution.
- G9.Respect training regulations, and actively participate fully and dynamically in contributing creatively to lessons.
- G10.Analyze and evaluate the achievements, limitations, and leadership experiences of the Party in the renovation process.
- G11.Analyze and evaluate the great victories of the Vietnamese revolution and the major lessons about the Party's leadership.
- G12.Apply the knowledge learned into practical work.
• Content:
- Chapter 1: The Birth of the Communist Party of Vietnam and Its Leadership in the Struggle for Power (1930-1945)
- Chapter 2: The Party Leads Two Resistance Wars, Achieving National Liberation and Unification of the Country (1945-1975)
- Chapter 3: The Party Leads the Entire Nation in the Transition to Socialism and the Implementation of the Renovation Process (1975-2018)

# COURSE: Đại số tuyến tính (MTH00035)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: MTH00035
- **English Name**: Linear Algebra
- **Vietnamese Name**: Đại số tuyến tính
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Dr. Lê Văn Luyện
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 2
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 25 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 116.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
(1) Learn about linear equations: Students will study the theory and techniques related to solving systems of linear equations. They will explore concepts such as matrix operations, row reduction, and Gaussian elimination to solve linear equations and analyze their solutions.
(2) Gain proficiency in matrix methods: Students will develop a strong understanding of matrices and their properties. They will learn how to perform operations on matrices, such as matrix addition, multiplication, and inversion. Additionally, they will explore applications of matrices in various fields.
(3) Explore analytical geometry: Students will delve into the study of vectors and their geometric interpretations. They will learn about vector operations, vector spaces, and vector equations. This knowledge will provide a foundation for understanding geometric concepts and their relationship to linear algebra.
(4) Understand linear transformations: Students will examine the concept of linear transformations and their properties. They will explore the relationship between linear transformations and matrices, as well as the applications of linear transformations.
By the end of the course, students will have a solid understanding of linear algebra concepts and their applications in mathematics, engineering, and the sciences. They will be equipped with the necessary knowledge to solve linear equations, perform matrix operations, analyze vector spaces, and comprehend the role of linear transformations. This knowledge will serve as a valuable foundation for their future studies.
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Chapter 1: Matrices and Systems of Linear Equations
- Chapter 2: Determinants
- Chapter 3: Vector Spaces
- Chapter 4: Linear Mappings
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Đại số tuyến tính và ứng dụng, Tập 1,Bùi Xuân Hải, Trần Ngọc Hội, Trịnh Thanh Đèo, Lê Văn Luyện,2009,
- [2]Giáo trình Đại số tuyến tính,Ngô Việt Trung,2001,
- [3]Đại số tuyến tính,Nguyễn Hữu Việt Hưng,2004,

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Recognize and explain the English terms related to the subject.
- G2.Practice effectively solving and reasoning through systems of linear equations.
- G3.Practice well with matrix calculations and know how to apply invertible matrices to solve matrix equations.
- G4.Practice calculating the determinant of a matrix to use it for solving systems of linear equations, finding the rank of a matrix, examining invertibility, and finding the inverse matrix.
- G8.Recognize ethical principles and responsibilities in learning.
- G9.Creative thinking and strong critical thinking skills. Maintain a serious attitude towards learning and self-study.
- G5.Apply knowledge of systems of linear equations, matrix operations, invertible matrices, and matrix determinants to describe the structure of vector spaces and related issues (subspaces, generating sets, linearly independent or dependent sets, finding bases for subspaces, vector coordinates relative to a basis, change of basis matrices, and coordinate transformation formulas).
- G6.Proficiently apply vector spaces to study linear mappings between vector spaces and related issues (basis of the product space, basis of the image space, matrix representation of linear mappings, formulas for changing matrix representations between bases, and finding linear mappings based on the image of a basis).
- G7.Effectively use knowledge of systems of linear equations, matrix determinants, and vector spaces to introduce eigenvalues, eigenspaces, eigenvectors, and the characteristic polynomial of square matrices. From there, define the concept of diagonalizable matrices, the necessary and sufficient conditions for diagonalizability, diagonalization algorithms, and applications of diagonalizable matrices.

- Chapter 1: Matrices and Systems of Linear Equations

- Chapter 3: Vector Spaces

# COURSE: Xác suất thống kê (MTH00044)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: MTH00044
- **English Name**: Applied Statistics for Engineers and Scientists
- **Vietnamese Name**: Xác suất thống kê
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Dr. Nguyễn Thị Mộng Ngọc
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 3
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 25 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 116.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
(1) Basic probability concepts: Students will learn the foundations of probability theory, including the concepts of sample space, events, probability axioms, and conditional probability. They will also study important concepts such as independence, Bayes' theorem, and the law of total probability.
(2) Random variables: Students will delve into the concept of random variables, understanding discrete and continuous random variables, probability mass functions, probability density functions, cumulative distribution functions, and moments of random variables.
(3) Common probability distributions: Students will explore widely used probability distributions, such as the binomial, Poisson, normal (Gaussian), and exponential distributions. They will understand the characteristics, properties, and applications of these distributions in various fields.
(4) Confidence intervals: Students will learn how to construct confidence intervals, which provide a range of plausible values for population parameters based on sample data. They will study methods for estimating population means, proportions, and other parameters, along with the interpretation and determination of confidence levels.
(5) Hypothesis testing: Students will understand the principles of hypothesis testing, including null and alternative hypotheses, Type I and Type II errors, significance levels, and p-values. They will learn hypothesis tests for population means, proportions, and variances.
(6) Regression and correlation: Students will explore the concepts of regression and correlation analysis. They will learn how to perform simple and multiple linear regression, interpret regression coefficients, assess model fit, and analyze the strength and direction of relationships using correlation coefficients.
By the end of the course, students will have a solid understanding of basic probability concepts, random variables, common probability distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and correlation. This knowledge will enable them to analyze and interpret data, make informed decisions based on statistical evidence, and apply statistical techniques in various fields.
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Chapter 0: Combinatorial Analysis
- Chapter 1: Basic Concepts of Probability
- Chapter 2: Random Variables
- Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
- Chapter 4: Estimation
- Chapter 5: Hypothesis Testing
- Chapter 6: Regression and Correlation
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Xác suất thống kê,Nguyễn Thị Mộng Ngọc,2018,
- [2]Bài Tập và Thực Hành Xác suất thống kê,Nguyễn Thị Mộng Ngọc,2018,
- [3]Giáo trình lý thuyết xác suất và thống kê toán. Bài tập Lý thuyết xác suất và thống kê toán,Trần Tuấn Điệp, Lý Hoàng Tú,1979,

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Knowledge: The course provides the fundamental knowledge and basic concepts of probability and statistics, serving as a foundation for subsequent subjects.
- G2.Skills: Apply probability and statistics to solve practical problems related to data analysis and presentation.
- G3.Attitude and diligence: The course helps students gain an initial understanding of probability and statistics and recognize the role of these fields in various scientific and real-life applications. This fosters a love for science and encourages a serious and proactive attitude toward learning.

# COURSE: Toán rời rạc (MTH00045)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: MTH00045
- **English Name**: Discrete Mathematics
- **Vietnamese Name**: Toán rời rạc
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Dr. Lê Văn Hợp
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 1
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 25 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 116.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
(1) Logic: Students will learn the principles of propositional logic and predicate logic. They will explore logical connectives, truth tables, logical equivalences, and methods of proof, enabling them to construct and analyze logical arguments.
(2) Sets and Relations: Students will study set theory, including operations on sets, set identities, and properties. They will also investigate various types of relations, such as equivalence relations and partial orders, and explore functions and their properties.
(3) Combinatorics: Students will explore counting principles and combinatorial techniques. They will learn about permutations, combinations, binomial coefficients, and the inclusion-exclusion principle. These concepts are fundamental for analyzing the number of possible outcomes in various scenarios.
(4) Graph Theory: Students will delve into graph theory, examining the properties and applications of graphs. They will learn about graph representations, connectivity, graph coloring, Eulerian and Hamiltonian paths, and spanning trees. Graph theory provides a powerful framework for solving problems in network design, optimization, and algorithm analysis.
Discrete Mathematics is a crucial course for students in disciplines such as computer science, information technology, and engineering, as it lays the groundwork for further studies in areas like algorithms, data structures, cryptography, and artificial intelligence.
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Chapter 1: Logic Foundations
- Chapter 2: Sets and Mappings
- Chapter 3: Counting Methods
- Chapter 4: Recursive Relations
- Chapter 5: Set of Integers
- Chapter 6: Relations on Sets
- Chapter 7: Boolean Functions
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Toán rời rạc,Nguyễn Hữu Anh,1999,
- [2]Discrete mathematicsand  its application,Kenneth h.  Rosen,,2012,
- [3]Discrete  mathematics,Richard johnsonbaugh,2005,

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Recognize and explain the English terms related to the subject.
- G2.Understand, know, and apply the foundational issues of logic, sets, and mappings.
- G3.Understand, know, and apply counting principles, combinatorial analysis, and recurrence relations in counting problems.
- G4.Understand, know, and practice integer algorithms.
- G5.Understand, know, and classify binary relations, order relations, and equivalence relations. Perform calculations in congruence relations.
- G6.Understand Boolean functions and implement algorithms to find the minimal polynomial form for Boolean functions.
- G7.Understand and adhere to ethical principles and responsibilities in learning.
- G8.Have creative thinking, strong critical thinking skills, and a serious attitude toward learning and self-study.
• Content:
- Chapter 1: Logic Foundations

# COURSE: Toán học tổ hợp (MTH00050)

• COURSE TOPICS:

- Rooted Trees

- Generating Function Coefficients
- Integer Partitions
- Exponential Generating Functions
- Midterm Exam
- Summation Method
- Solving Recurrence Relations Using Generating Functions

- Inclusion-Exclusion Principle
- Rook Polynomial
- Chapter 7. Advanced Counting
- Catalan Numbers
- Stirling Numbers of the Second Kind
- Bell Numbers

- G5.Construct generating functions and use them to solve counting problems.
- G6.Apply the principle of inclusion-exclusion and the polynomial of rooks to solve counting problems.
- G7.Explain the definitions of some advanced counting principles.

- Optimizing multivariable function
- Calculation method for optimization
- Dynamic models

- Analysis of dynamic models
- Simulation of dynamic models
- Some applied math problems of dynamic modeling
- Probabilistic models

- Random samples
- Simulate probabilistic models
- Some applied math problems of probability modeling
- Review
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Understand the relevant basic math knowledge to AI.
- G2.Use math methods for AI.
- G3.Understand and use discrete structures: combinatorics, graphs.
- G4.Understand and develop function optimal models.
- G5.Understand and develop dynamical models.
- G6.Understand and develop probabilistic models.
• Content:
- Introduction to discrete structures:
- combinatorics and
- graph structures
- Graph search algorithms:
- Depth First Search
- Breadth First Search (BFT)
- Connected Graphs
- Connectivity in graphs
- Complete Graphs
- Strongly Connected Components
- Some applied math problems of graphs
- Function optimal models
- Optimizing a 1-variable function
- Optimizing multivariable function
- Calculation method for optimization
- Dynamic models
- Introduction to dynamic models
- Analysis of dynamic models
- Simulation of dynamic models
- Some applied math problems of dynamic modeling
- Probabilistic models
- Introduction to probabilistic models
- Random samples
- Simulate probabilistic models
- Some applied math problems of probability modeling
- Review
• Examination forms: Final examination, Homework
• Study and examination requirements:
- All students are responsible for reading and following strictly the regulations and policies of the school and university.
- Students who are absent for more than 3 theory sessions are not allowed to take the exams.
- For any kind of cheating and plagiarism, students will be graded 0 for the course. The incident is then submitted to the school and university for further review.
- Students are encouraged to form study groups to discuss on the topics. However, individual work must be done and submitted on your own.
- Regular class attendance is strongly advised and is necessary for students to fully grasp many of the course concepts.
- Please be on time to class.
- If you miss a class session, it will be your responsibility to find out the materials that were covered.
- Students in attendance are expected to be active participants in the course. The participation includes contributing to class discussions, providing insight into the class discussion topics, raising questions, and relating class material to personal experiences and other course topics.
- Moodle and e-mail will be used to communicate with students and disseminate materials and assignments throughout the course. So, students should check Moodle and their email at least once per day.
- When sending e-mail to the instructor, please begin the Subject of the message with the following format: [Course ID]. For example, "[CSC10001] Ask for additional materials".
• Reading list:
- [1]Mathematics for Machine Learning,Marc Peter Deisenroth, A. Aldo Faisal, Cheng Soon Ong,2021,CAMBRIDGE UNIVERSITY PRESS

# COURSE: Nhập môn công nghệ thông tin (CSC00004)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: CSC00004
- **English Name**: Introduction to Information Technology
- **Vietnamese Name**: Nhập môn công nghệ thông tin
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Lê Ngọc Thành
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 1
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 25 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 116.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Introduce an overview of the development process, the role of IT in life. Learn about computer classification, the basic architecture of a computer. Introduce the characteristics and role of software in the computer.
- Introduce primary hardware devices such as ports and circuits to peripheral devices such as RAM, hard drives, etc... in computers. Identify each device's characteristics, roles, and meanings and how they work together.
- Principle of storing information in computers such as number (integer and floating point), text, images, sounds, ... Basic operations on the counting system.
- How the CPU executes a low-level program. How the CPU works with other devices to make a request. The relationship between low-level languages and high-level programming languages.
- Operating system (OS): characteristics, roles, and development process on all computer platforms. The basic architecture and components of the operating system
- Computer network: transmission principles, connection protocols, network types, security. Supported devices and software. Computer security such as how to attack and how to protect the computer.
- Introduction to the major of Computer networks at FIT-HCMUS.
- Database system: roles, meanings, applications. Database management systems.
- Introduction to the major of Information Systems at FIT-HCMUS
- Introduction to artificial intelligence, machine learning, and applications.
- Introduction to the major of Computer Science at FIT-HCMUS
- Software engineering: The Software Engineering Discipline, The Software Life Cycle, Development Phases, Software Engineering Methodologies, Documentation, Project Management.
- Introduction to the major of Software Engineering at FIT-HCMUS
- Professional ethics such as copyright laws, privacy and legal documents related to IT. Rules and regulations in university and company.
- Introduction to FIT-HCMUS
- Introduction to IT certificates
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Computer science: reflection on the field, reflections from the field, Committee on the Fundamentals of Computer Science,,2004,National Academies Press
- [2]Computer science: an overview, 12th edition,Gleen Brookshear, Dennis Brylow,2014,
- [3]Computers are your future, Introductory, 12th Edition,Catherine Laberta,2011,
- [4]Ethics for the Information Age, 7th Edition,Mike Quinn,2016,

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Implement and present some topics related to general knowledge career orientation in teamwork. Demonstrate critical thinking and creative thinking in the process of implementing topics.
- G2.Present the meaning, role, status of hardware devices and software. Apply software technology platforms to collaborate, present, share, and backup data.
- G3.Explain the data storage platform in a computer and describe the roles of database and database administration systems.
- G4.Identify the role of the operating system, the development process, and distinguish different types of operating systems
- G5.Present basic knowledge about computer networks, the internet, network protocols, devices, connection issues, and security issues
- G6.Summarize the main characteristics of artificial intelligence and related fields and describe some emerging technologies
- G7.List some important rules and regulations in the university environment and in the company environment. Explain some characteristics of professional ethics
• Content:
- Introduce an overview of the development process, the role of IT in life. Learn about computer classification, the basic architecture of a computer. Introduce the characteristics and role of software in the computer.
- Introduce primary hardware devices such as ports and circuits to peripheral devices such as RAM, hard drives, etc... in computers. Identify each device's characteristics, roles, and meanings and how they work together.
- Principle of storing information in computers such as number (integer and floating point), text, images, sounds, ... Basic operations on the counting system.
- How the CPU executes a low-level program. How the CPU works with other devices to make a request. The relationship between low-level languages and high-level programming languages.
- Operating system (OS): characteristics, roles, and development process on all computer platforms. The basic architecture and components of the operating system
- Computer network: transmission principles, connection protocols, network types, security. Supported devices and software. Computer security such as how to attack and how to protect the computer.
- Introduction to the major of Computer networks at FIT-HCMUS.
- Database system: roles, meanings, applications. Database management systems.
- Introduction to the major of Information Systems at FIT-HCMUS
- Introduction to artificial intelligence, machine learning, and applications.
- Introduction to the major of Computer Science at FIT-HCMUS
- Software engineering: The Software Engineering Discipline, The Software Life Cycle, Development Phases, Software Engineering Methodologies, Documentation, Project Management.
- Introduction to the major of Software Engineering at FIT-HCMUS
- Professional ethics such as copyright laws, privacy and legal documents related to IT. Rules and regulations in university and company.
- Introduction to FIT-HCMUS
- Introduction to IT certificates
• Examination forms:

Software Engineering at FIT-HCMUS
- Professional ethics such as copyright laws, privacy and legal documents related to IT. Rules and regulations in university and company.
- Introduction to FIT-HCMUS
- Introduction to IT certificates

- All students are responsible for reading and following strictly the regulations and policies of the school and university.

# COURSE: Cấu trúc dữ liệu và giải thuật (CSC10004)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: CSC10004
- **English Name**: Data Structures and Algorithms
- **Vietnamese Name**: Cấu trúc dữ liệu và giải thuật
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Lê Ngọc Thành
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 3
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 25 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 116.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Introduction to DSA
- Recursion
- Algorithm efficiency: Big-O
- Search algorithms: Linear search and Binary search
- Sorting algorithms: O(n2) algorithms
- Sorting algorithms: O(nlog2n) algorithms
- Sorting algorithms: O(n) algorithms (Radix Sort)
- Reviews: Linked lists, Stack, Queue and Priority Queue
- Tree structures: General tree, Binary search tree
- Midterm Examination
- Tree structures: B-tree with optional extensions covering Red-black tree, AA tree, 2-3 tree, 2-3-4 tree
- Graph structure: Basic concepts and graph traversals (Breadth-first search, Depth-first search).
- Graph structure: Topological sorting, Minimum spanning tree, and Shortest path
- Hashing, Hash table with collision resolution techniques
- Reviews
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Data Abstraction and Problem Solving with C++: Walls and Mirrors (Seventh Edition),Frank M. Carrano, Timothy Henry,2014,Pearson
- [2]Introduction to Algorithms (Fourth Edition),Thomas H. Cormen, Charles E. Leiserson, Ronald L. Rivest and Clifford Stein,2022,MIT press
- [3]The Algorithm Design Manual (Second Edition),Steven S. Skiena,2008,Springer

- G1.Interpret the fundamental concepts and terminologies of Data structures and Algorithms in the given context
- G2.Analyze the algorithms and data structures in terms of time and space complexities using asymptotic notations
- G3.Choose the algorithms and data structures following the problem requirements (e.g., data size and distribution, a tradeoff between execution time and storage)
- G4.Implement the algorithms and data structures using C/C++ programming language
- G5.Promote personal aptitudes for logical thinking and communication
- G1.Interpret the fundamental concepts and terminologies of Data structures and Algorithms in the given context

# COURSE: Cơ sở dữ liệu (CSC10006)

• COURSE TOPICS:

- Relational data model

- Relational Calculus
- Functional dependency and Normal forms

- Integrity constraints
- Integrity constraints (cont)
- Review

• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:

- G5.Use database languages to manipulate the databases
- G6.Discover the quality of a given database scheme and practice the methods to normalize a database scheme
- G7.Describe and define integrity constraints in a database
- G8.Describe the future trends in database systems research
- G9.Set up and manipulate the database using a relational database management system

- Entity relationship model
- Relational data model

• COURSE TOPICS:
- Overview of computer knowledge, data represented on the computer

- (Extend IA32 to 64 bits)
- Running program on a system

- I/O System
- Review

# COURSE: Quy hoạch tuyến tính (CSC10104)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: CSC10104
- **English Name**: Linear Programming
- **Vietnamese Name**: Quy hoạch tuyến tính
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Lê Phúc Lữ
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 6
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 25 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 116.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
The course focuses on introducing students to the concepts, some mathematical models, applied problems and simulations in Python to solve problems related to the foundations of linear algebra and linear programming.
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Introduce to the important subjects, tools:
- About some optimization problems
- Reminds about the basic concepts of linear algebra: vectors, matrices
- Introduction about some practical applications of the linear algebra
- Introduce the simulation by some Python software package as numpy, scipy, simpy, ...
- Introduction to the linear programming problems
- Concept
- Introduction
- Components, terms
- Illustrated problem
- Solution method
- Geometric methods for 2D problems.
- Introduce about simplex algorithm
- Finding the basic feasible solution
- Simplex table and interations
- Optimal sign of simplex method
- Basic problems in linear algebra
- • Deterministic calculation
- • Solve the equation of 2 unknowns
- • Gaussian method
- • Matrix product
- • Solve equations of many unknowns
- Some advanced content on linear programming
- • Big M technique in monomorphic method
- • The concept of duality
- • Computational processing for dual problem
- Review: some questions for the midterm test
- About linear algebra and linear programming
- • PCA method
- • SVM method
- • Solve linear programming problems with a software package based on Python.
- Application of linear programming
- • Integer programming problem
- • Introduce transportation problem: introduction, three ways to find bases, algorithms.
- • Other applications in other problems: graph, game, coding theory, ...
- Review 1: about linear algebra
- Review 2: about linear programming
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]Linear Programming - Foundations and Extensions,Robert J.Vanderbei,2020,Springer

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Know some terms in the optimization problems, linear algebra, the basic components such as vectors, matrices, ...
Understanding what is the linear programming problem and related concepts.
- G2.Solve basic problems in linear algebra such as determining determinant, Gauss method, matrix product, solving system of equations with many unknowns, ...
Know how to solve the problem of linear programming by geometry, simplex algorithms; Understand and explainable the terms in the linear programming.
- G3.Know some practical applications of linear algebra and simulation using numpy, scipy, simpy software packages.
Know the advanced content of linear programming: the big M technique to find the basis in the simplex algorithm, the concept of duality and relate to the original article.
- G4.Study the advanced contents on linear algebra such as PCA, SVM and solving linear programming problem on Python.
Know how to apply linear programming to problems: integer programming, transportation problems, game theory.
• Content:
- Introduce to the important subjects, tools:
- About some optimization problems
- Reminds about the basic concepts of linear algebra: vectors, matrices
- Introduction about some practical applications of the linear algebra
- Introduce the simulation by some Python software package as numpy, scipy, simpy, ...
- Introduction to the linear programming problems
- Concept
- Introduction
- Components, terms
- Illustrated problem
- Solution method
- Geometric methods for 2D problems.
- Introduce about simplex algorithm
- Finding the basic feasible solution
- Simplex table and interations
- Optimal sign of simplex method
- Basic problems in linear algebra
- • Deterministic calculation
- • Solve the equation of 2 unknowns
- • Gaussian method
- • Matrix product
- • Solve equations of many unknowns
- Some advanced content on linear programming
- • Big M technique in monomorphic method
- • The concept of duality
- • Computational processing for dual problem
- Review: some questions for the midterm test
- About linear algebra and linear programming
- • PCA method
- • SVM method
- • Solve linear programming problems with a software package based on Python.
- Application of linear programming
- • Integer programming problem
- • Introduce transportation problem: introduction, three ways to find bases, algorithms.
- • Other applications in other problems: graph, game, coding theory, ...
- Review 1: about linear algebra
- Review 2: about linear programming

# COURSE: Nhập môn tư duy thuật toán (CSC10105)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: CSC10105
- **English Name**: Introduction to Algorithmic Thinking
- **Vietnamese Name**: Nhập môn tư duy thuật toán
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Dr. Nguyễn Thanh Hùng

- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 25 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 116.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
During the course, students are also trained in their coding skills, their thinking mindset, and coding interviewing skills such as algorithm explanation, complexities optimization, etc.
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Introduction to Competitive Programming, Algorithms and Complexity
- Review variations of simple data structures.
- Introduction of advanced Data Structures
- Recursion and Dynamic Programming Algorithms
- String Processing and Greedy Algorithms
- Mathematical Algorithms and Complexity Optimization
- Geometric Computation
- Introduction to Graph Theory
- Introduction to Game Theory
- Coding Interviewing Process Introduction and Simulation
- Review
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:

- G1.Understanding complexities, in terms of space and time.
- G2.Learning essential data structures.
- G3.Studying a variety of algorithms in many topics related to competitive programming and coding interview questions.
- G4.Training and practicing to gain problem analyzing, explaining, optimizing skill sets.
- G5.Getting used to coding interviewing processes.
- G6.Experimenting coding interviewing processes.

# COURSE: Thuật toán tổ hợp và ứng dụng (CSC10106)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: CSC10106
- **English Name**: Combinatorial Algorithms and Applications
- **Vietnamese Name**: Thuật toán tổ hợp và ứng dụng
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Dr. Nguyễn Thanh Hùng
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 6
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 37.5 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 25 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 116.25 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Introduction to Graph Theory and Combinatorial Algorithms
- Graph Representation and Applications
- Shortest path finding Algorithms
- Minimum Spanning Trees
- Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) and Topological Sorting
- Graph-Based Data Analysis
- Large Graphs and Efficiency Challenges
- Graph Algorithms in Optimization Problems
- Research and Advanced Topics in Graph Theory
- Graph-based Data Analysis in Social Networks
- Review
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]An Introduction to Combinatorics and Graph Theory,David Guichard,2022,Creative Commons
- [2]Graph Theory,Reinhard Diestel,2010,
- [3]Introduction to Graph Theory,Douglas B. West,2007,
- [4]Introduction to Algorithms,T. Cormen, C. Leiserson, R. Rivest,2001,
- [5]Combinatorial Optimization: Algorithms and Complexity,Christos H. Papadimitriou,1998,

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G5.Prepare students for advanced research on large-scale data analysis using graph representations.
- G1.Apply algorithms from graph theory to solve practical problems across different domains.
- G2.Recognize problems that can be addressed using graph theory.
- G3.Select appropriate graph representations based on the problem context.
- G4.Provide students with an understanding of the practical uses of graph theory in various fields such as traffic flow management, telephone network construction, and social network data analysis.
- G6.Develop core algorithms for software applications related to navigation, pathfinding, and process optimization
• Content:
- Introduction to Graph Theory and Combinatorial Algorithms
- Graph Representation and Applications
- Shortest path finding Algorithms
- Minimum Spanning Trees
- Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) and Topological Sorting
- Graph-Based Data Analysis
- Large Graphs and Efficiency Challenges
- Graph Algorithms in Optimization Problems
- Research and Advanced Topics in Graph Theory
- Graph-based Data Analysis in Social Networks
- Review

# COURSE: Thực tập thực tế (CSC10107)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: CSC10107
- **English Name**: Internship
- **Vietnamese Name**: Thực tập thực tế
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Văn Chí Nam
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 6
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 25 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 50 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 117.5 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
(student) interns will be able to take part in the company’s real projects. They can discover organization structures, roles and responsibilities within that structure, the processes applied at the company; demonstrate their own knowledge, techniques and professional skills; and comprehend the company culture or etiquette rules. Through the course, students could make more connections with people in the same professional field, gain insight into a career path for their future job, and build their professional manners and attitude to adapt in the business and industrial environment.
Students can choose which company to take the internship by their own based on the “Call for Internship” posts in the official faculty or university websites, or in other websites, media, etc. The company is not limit in Ho Chi Minh city or in Viet Nam. Students are encouraged to take their internship in foreign countries.
The internship is required related to information technology field (for examples, application/system design, application/system analysis, application/system building, application/system testing, etc.), and not limit to any programming languages, frameworks, operation systems, types of application. The internship must be full time and not less than 12 weeks.
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Before the start of the internship:
- Students are required to fill in a form with the information related to an industry or a professional organization outlining the duties and expectations during the internship. The information also includes start date, end date, hours to be worked, and name, email, phone of the on-site supervisor. After finishing the internship:
- Students are requested to write a report on what they have done during their internship. As a recommendation, this report should have (not limit to) following parts: overview of the company; information about the internship, the project, intern role in the project; ways of communications; models/solutions/techniques applied in the project; (success and/or failure) lessons learned from this internship, etc. This report should be formatted as a technical report with cover, table of contents, references, etc.
- Also, students have to declare the following statement in the report: "I declare that this internship report is my own work and does not involve in plagiarism or collusion. I accept heavy penalty for any cheating or plagiarism
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:

# COURSE: Thực tập dự án tốt nghiệp (CSC10204)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: CSC10204
- **English Name**: Capstone Projects
- **Vietnamese Name**: Thực tập dự án tốt nghiệp
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Assoc. Prof. Nguyễn Văn Vũ
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 7
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 0 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 150 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 180 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
Topics for the capstone project are proposed by either students or project suppervisors, and are approved by suppervisors. The topics can be either research-oriented or application-oriented. In the research-oriented project, students need to propose a relevant research topic (based on recommendations of a project supervisor), carry out necessary research activities, write a report, and present the project in front of a committee. The research work may lead to publications in a scientific conference or journal. In the application-oriented project, students in collaboration with a project supervisor propose and develop a software application, write a report to describe the application along with the process of developing it, and present the project in front of a committee. The application should have the potential to be applied in practice and is possibly deployed for real use.
Students on each team are assessed and graded by the committee using the course’s predefined rubrics. They are required to meet a designated reviewer to present their work and answer any questions the reviewer has. They then to present the project in front of the committee to defend their work. The final grade will be calculated from the grades given by the reviewer, the supervisor, and the committee members.
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Topic registration.
- Project proposal submission.
- Project report submission for defense.
- Project defense with the defense committee.
- Report revision and final submission after the defense.
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]The only grammar book you'll ever need: A one-stop source for every writing assignment,Thurman, Susan, and Larry Shea,2003,Adams Media
- [2]Writing for Computer Science (4th. ed.).,Justin Zobel,2014,Springer
- [3]How to write a great research paper,Simon Peyton Jones,,Microsoft Research, Cambridge
- [4]How to Write a SIGGRAPH Paper: A Guide to Choosing a Good Research Topic, Doing the Research, and Writing It Up,David Salesin, Adobe,2012,SIGGRAPH Tutorial

## LEARNING OBJECTIVES (CLOs)
- G1.Analyze a sufficiently complex problem, generate ideas and requirements for an appropriate solution to the problem
- G2.Apply appropriate knowledge, skills, techniques and tools to design, implement and evaluate the proposed solution (either a scientific solution or a software application)
- G3.Create a technical report to communicate the proposed solution in detail
- G4.Defend the project through a poster presentation to a reviewer and the committee
- G5.Practice project management activities
- G6.Practice teamwork
• Content:
- Topic registration.
- Project proposal submission.
- Project report submission for defense.
- Project defense with the defense committee.
- Report revision and final submission after the defense.

# COURSE: Khóa luận tốt nghiệp (CSC10251)

## GENERAL INFORMATION
- **Course ID**: CSC10251
- **English Name**: Undergraduate Thesis
- **Vietnamese Name**: Khóa luận tốt nghiệp
- **Responsible Person**: for the module: Assoc. Prof. Nguyễn Văn Vũ
- **Semester**: (s) in which the module is taught: 7
- **Workload**: (incl. contact hours, self-study hours): Hours for lecture: ~ 0 hours Hours for laboratory session: ~ 250 hours Hours for self-study, examination preparation, other activities: ~ 300 hours

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
The thesis project can be either research-oriented or application-oriented. In the research-oriented project, students need to propose a relevant research topic (based on recommendations of a thesis supervisor), carry out necessary research activities, write a thesis, and present the thesis in front of a thesis committee. The research work may lead to publications in a scientific conference or journal. In the application-oriented project, students in collaboration with a thesis supervisor propose and develop a software application, write a thesis to describe the application along with the process of developing it, and present the thesis in front of a thesis committee. The application should have the potential to be applied in practice and is possibly deployed for real use.
Students on each team are assessed and graded by the thesis committee using the course’s predefined rubrics. They are required to meet a designated thesis reviewer to present their work and answer any questions the reviewer has. They then to present the thesis in front of the committee to defend their work. The final grade will be calculated from the grades given by the reviewer, the supervisor, and the committee members
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Topic registration.
- Project proposal submission.
- Project report submission for defense.
- Project defense with the defense committee.
- Report revision and final submission after the defense.
• COURSE GOALS: On successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
• REQUIRED AND RECOMMENDED READING:
- [1]The only grammar book you'll ever need: A one-stop source for every writing assignment,Thurman, Susan, and Larry Shea,2003,Adams Media
- [2]Writing for Computer Science (4th. ed.).,Justin Zobel,2014,Springer

- G1.Analyze a sufficiently complex problem, generate ideas and requirements for an appropriate solution to the problem

- G3.Create a technical report to communicate the proposed solution in detail
- G4.Defend the project through a poster presentation to a reviewer and the committee
- G5.Practice project management activities
- G6.Practice teamwork

## COURSE DESCRIPTION
The course is performed in the form of thesis projects. Each project is carried out by up to two students who are expected to complete the project within 6 months. The thesis project is expected to be the culmination of students’ years of undergraduate study. They need to apply all knowledge, skills, and experience gained from all courses that they have already taken to carry out work necessary to complete the thesis project. The project is also expected to be sufficiently large in size and complexity as compared with other projects taken during their undergraduate study.
The thesis project can be either research-oriented or application-oriented. In the research-oriented project, students need to propose a relevant research topic (based on recommendations of a thesis supervisor), carry out necessary research activities, write a thesis, and present the thesis in front of a thesis committee. The research work may lead to publications in a scientific conference or journal. In the application-oriented project, students in collaboration with a thesis supervisor propose and develop a software application, write a thesis to describe the application along with the process of developing it, and present the thesis in front of a thesis committee. The application should have the potential to be applied in practice and is possibly deployed for real use.
Students on each team are assessed and graded by the thesis committee using the course’s predefined rubrics. They are required to meet a designated thesis reviewer to present their work and answer any questions the reviewer has. They then to present the thesis in front of the committee to defend their work. The final grade will be calculated from the grades given by the reviewer, the supervisor, and the committee members.
• COURSE TOPICS:
- Topic registration.
- Project proposal submission.
- Project report submission for defense.
